title: कुबेर द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री कुबेर द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम् ॥

प्रथमं कुबेरमित्युक्तं द्वितीयं धनदं तथा।
तृतीयं यक्षराजं च चतुर्थं निधिपालकम्॥१॥

पञ्चमं वैश्रवणं च षष्ठं ऐलविलं विदुः।
सप्तमं धनाध्यक्षं च अष्टमं रत्नगर्भकम्॥२॥

नवमं शंकरसखं दशमं पुण्यजनेश्वरम्।
एकादशं अलकापतिं द्वादशं नवनिधीश्वरम्॥३॥

द्वादशैतानि नामानि कुबेरस्य महात्मनः।
प्रातः काले पठेद्यस्तु धनधान्यसमृद्धिभाक्॥४॥

दारिद्र्यं नश्यते तस्य निधयः सन्निधिं गताः।
सदा लक्ष्मीः स्थिरा तस्य कुबेरप्रसादतः॥५॥

॥ इति श्री कुबेर द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रं सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Kubera Dwadash Nama Stotram ||

Prathamam Kuberam-ityuktam Dvitiyam Dhanadam Tatha.
Tritiyam Yaksha-rajam Cha Chaturtham Nidhi-palakam.||1||

Panchamam Vaishrayanam Cha Shashtham Ailavilam Viduh.
Saptamam Dhana-adhyaksham Cha Ashtamam Ratna-garbhakam.||2||

Navamam Shankara-sakham Dashamam Punya-janeshvaram.
Ekadasham Alaka-patim Dvadasham Nava-nidhishvaram.||3||

Dvadashai-tani Namani Kuberasya Mahatmanah.
Pratah Kale Pathed-yastu Dhana-dhanya-samriddhi-bhak.||4||

Daridriyam Nashyate Tasya Nidhayah Sannidhim Gatah.
Sada Lakshmih Sthira Tasya Kubera-prasadatah.||5||

|| Iti Shri Kubera Dwadash Nama Stotram Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning: प्रथम नाम कुबेर, दूसरा धनद, तीसरा यक्षराज, चौथा निधिपालक। पाँचवाँ वैश्रवण, छठा ऐलविल, सातवाँ धनाध्यक्ष, आठवाँ रत्नगर्भक। नवाँ शंकरसख, दसवाँ पुण्यजनेश्वर, ग्यारहवाँ अलकापति, बारहवाँ नवनिधीश्वर। कुबेर के इन बारह नामों का प्रातःकाल पाठ करने से धन-धान्य समृद्धि होती है, दरिद्रता नष्ट होती है, नवनिधियाँ समीप आती हैं और लक्ष्मी सदा स्थिर रहती हैं।
benefits: Twelve names of Kubera, the celestial treasurer, for wealth
Traditionally recited for removal of poverty and financial difficulties
Attracts the nine treasures (nava-nidhi) to the devotee
Traditionally recited to invoke lakshmi (prosperity) remains stable in the household
Best recited on Dhanteras, Diwali, and every morning
description: Among the most cherished prayers to Lord Kubera, Kubera Dwadash Nama Stotram (कुबेर द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम्) stands as a devotional hymn of praise. This revered text originates from Vishnu Purana. Chanting this stotra with devotion is said to attract the nine treasures (nava-nidhi) to the devotee as well as destroy poverty and financial difficulties. Additionally, it is known to twelve names of Kubera, the celestial treasurer, for wealth. For best results, devotees chant this on Thursday (Guruvar) and Friday (Shukravar) and during Dhanteras and Diwali. With 5 verses and a reading time of about 3 minutes, this prayer is ideal for both daily worship and special occasions. On this page, you can read the complete Kubera Dwadash Nama Stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Recite Kubera Dwadash Nama - 12 names of Lord Kubera in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning for wealth, prosperity, and removal of poverty.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Kubera Dwadash Nama Stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: The Kubera Dwadash Nama Stotram (कुबेर द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम्) is a short five-verse Sanskrit hymn that lists the twelve sacred names (dvadasha = twelve, nama = name) of Lord Kubera — the celestial treasurer (Dhanapati), king of the yakshas (Yaksharaja), guardian of the northern direction (Uttara-dik-pala), keeper of the nine treasures (Nava-nidhi), and lord of the city of Alakapuri on Mount Kailasa. The twelve names enumerated are: (1) Kubera, (2) Dhanada (giver of wealth), (3) Yaksharaja (king of yakshas), (4) Nidhipalaka (keeper of treasures), (5) Vaishravana (son of sage Vishrava), (6) Ailavila (son of Ilavila), (7) Dhanadhyaksha (overseer of wealth), (8) Ratnagarbha (womb of jewels), (9) Shankarasakha (friend of Shiva), (10) Punyajaneshvara (lord of the meritorious yakshas), (11) Alakapati (lord of Alaka), and (12) Navanidhishvara (lord of the nine treasures). Tradition places the text in the Vishnu Purana wealth-stotra corpus. (Source: preserved in classical tradition; Vishnu Purana Kubera-stuti.)

seoFaqs.question: Who is Lord Kubera and why are his twelve names so important?
seoFaqs.answer: Kubera is the Hindu deva of wealth, the celestial treasurer (Dhanapati), commander of the yaksha and guhyaka clans, half-brother of Ravana, and the appointed dik-pala (directional guardian) of the northern quarter (uttara-dik). According to the Vishnu Purana and Mahabharata, he was originally the son of sage Vishrava (hence Vaishravana) and Ilavila (hence Ailavila), and through severe tapasya he obtained from Brahma the lordship of all wealth, the Pushpaka Vimana (which Ravana later seized), and the city of Alakapuri on Mount Kailasa adjoining Shiva's residence (hence his epithet Shankara-sakha, friend of Shiva). The twelve names systematically invoke each aspect of his wealth-bestowing power — as treasurer, jewel-source, keeper of the nine nidhis (Padma, Mahapadma, Shankha, Makara, Kachchhapa, Mukunda, Kunda, Nila, Kharva) — making the dvadasha-nama recitation one of the most concise and powerful wealth-mantras in Hindu tradition. (Sources: Vishnu Purana; Mahabharata Sabha Parva.)

seoFaqs.question: When should the Kubera Dwadash Nama Stotram be recited for maximum benefit?
seoFaqs.answer: The Kubera Dwadash Nama Stotram is most powerfully recited at pratahkala (early morning, as explicitly stated in the phalashruti of verse 4 — Pratah kale pathed-yastu) facing north (Kubera's direction) before any other activity, and especially on Thursday (Guruvar — the day of Brihaspati, deva-guru, governing wealth and prosperity) and Friday (Shukravar — the day of Lakshmi). The supreme occasions are: Dhanteras (Kartik Krishna Trayodashi — the festival when Kubera is worshipped alongside Dhanvantari and new gold/silver is purchased), Diwali (Lakshmi Puja night — when Kubera and Lakshmi are jointly worshipped at the household tijori/safe), Akshaya Tritiya (Vaishakha Shukla Tritiya — the day of inexhaustible wealth), Kojagari Purnima, the start of any new business or financial year, and on the day of taking a loan or settling a debt. Daily morning recitation is the classical household practice. (Sources: Drikpanchang wealth festivals; Lakshmi-Kubera puja vidhi.)

seoFaqs.question: What does the phalashruti (verse 4-5) promise to regular reciters?
seoFaqs.answer: The phalashruti at the end of the Kubera Dwadash Nama Stotram (verses 4-5) makes four explicit promises: (1) Dhana-dhanya-samriddhi-bhak — the reciter becomes endowed with the abundance of wealth (dhana) and grain (dhanya) — that is, both monetary wealth and the foundational sustenance of life; (2) Daridryam nashyate tasya — his poverty (daridrya) is destroyed; (3) Nidhayah sannidhim gatah — the nine nidhis (treasures) come and dwell in his proximity; (4) Sada Lakshmih sthira tasya Kubera-prasadatah — through the grace of Kubera, Lakshmi (prosperity) becomes permanently stable (sthira) in his household. The fourth promise is especially significant — many people obtain wealth temporarily but cannot retain it; the Kubera Dwadasha Nama is specifically credited with the stability (sthirata) of wealth, addressing the chanchala (fickle) nature of Lakshmi. (Source: Kubera Dwadash Nama Stotram phalashruti verses 4-5.)

seoFaqs.question: What are the Nava-Nidhi (nine treasures) of Kubera referenced in the twelfth name?
seoFaqs.answer: The twelfth name in the stotram is Nava-nidhishvara (lord of the nine treasures). The Nava-nidhi are the nine personified celestial treasures placed under Kubera's stewardship, enumerated principally in the Vishnu Purana, Linga Purana and Markandeya Purana: (1) Padma — the lotus treasure; (2) Mahapadma — the great lotus treasure; (3) Shankha — the conch treasure; (4) Makara — the alligator-emblem treasure; (5) Kachchhapa — the tortoise treasure; (6) Mukunda — the Mukunda-named treasure; (7) Kunda — the jasmine treasure; (8) Nila — the sapphire treasure; (9) Kharva — the dwarf-named treasure. Each nidhi governs a particular kind of wealth — agricultural produce, gold, gems, livestock, weapons, knowledge, etc. The phalashruti promises that these nidhis come into the proximity (sannidhi) of one who recites the dvadasha-nama — meaning that all nine streams of wealth flow towards the devotee. (Source: Vishnu Purana; Linga Purana Nava-nidhi tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: How is the Kubera Dwadash Nama different from the Lakshmi Ashtottara Shatanamavali?
seoFaqs.answer: Both are name-recitation hymns invoking wealth-bestowing deities, but they have different scope and complement each other in classical wealth-puja. The Kubera Dwadash Nama is a short twelve-name hymn to Kubera, the male masculine deva who is the treasurer (the one who keeps and distributes wealth) — recited briefly each morning for daily prosperity and for stability of acquired wealth. The Lakshmi Ashtottara Shatanamavali is a longer 108-name hymn to Devi Lakshmi, the feminine source-power of all auspiciousness, prosperity, beauty and good fortune — recited especially on Friday and during Lakshmi pujas (Diwali, Varalakshmi Vrata, Kojagari Purnima) to invoke the very arrival and presence of prosperity. The classical formula is to recite both: Lakshmi to bring wealth, Kubera to keep and grow it. The Diwali and Dhanteras puja explicitly worships Lakshmi-Kubera together as the feminine and masculine faces of artha-purushartha. (Source: Lakshmi-Kubera puja paddhati.)
